methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

path: 7. pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn) — STATE UPDATE AFTER EXTERNAL CALL
8. pool.reserveValidatorToken -= uint128(amountOut) — STATE UPDATE AFTER EXTERNAL CALL
data_flows.trust_changes: Caller-supplied token addresses are trusted without validation; any address can be passed as userToken/validatorToken
validation_points: reserve sufficiency check before outgoing transfer
exposure_risks: No validation that tokens are legitimate ERC20 contracts
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL REENTRANCY: userToken.transferFrom callback fires BEFORE pool reserves are updated. Attacker can reenter executeFeeSwap and drain validatorToken reserves because the reserve check still passes with the old (higher) reserveValidatorToken value.
Fee-on-transfer tokens cause reserve accounting mismatch: amountIn is credited to reserves but actual tokens received may be less
No check that amountIn > 0; zero-amount swaps waste gas and emit misleading events

path: 1. Caller invokes rebalanceSwap(userToken, validatorToken, amountOut, to)
3. amountIn = (amountOut * N) / SCALE + 1 — fixed-rate with rounding up
7. pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) — STATE UPDATE BEFORE TRANSFER
8. pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) — STATE UPDATE BEFORE TRANSFER
transformations: amountIn = amountOut * 9985 / 10000 + 1 (0.15% fee with ceil rounding)
data_flows.trust_changes: Optimistic state update: reserves are modified before actual token transfers occur
manipulation_risks: HIGH REENTRANCY: State is updated BEFORE transferFrom. If validatorToken has callbacks (ERC777), attacker can reenter burn or other functions while reserves reflect tokens that haven't actually arrived. Contract's actual validatorToken balance is less than reserveValidatorToken. Reentering burn would extract tokens based on inflated reserves.
Fee-on-transfer tokens: fewer tokens arrive than amountIn, but reserves reflect the full amountIn

path: 1. Caller invokes mint(userToken, validatorToken, amountValidatorToken, to)
5. Branch: first deposit vs subsequent deposit
5a. First deposit: liquidity = amountValidatorToken / 2 - MIN_LIQUIDITY; totalSupply += MIN_LIQUIDITY (locked, unowned)
5b. Subsequent: denom = reserveValidatorToken + (N * reserveUserToken / SCALE); liquidity = amountValidatorToken * totalSupply / denom
9. pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken) — STATE UPDATE AFTER EXTERNAL CALL
transformations: Subsequent: proportional to effective pool value
data_flows.trust_changes: LP tokens minted to arbitrary 'to' address; no overflow check on reserveValidatorToken + amountValidatorToken (relies on Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic giving opaque revert)
exposure_risks: LP tokens are non-transferable (no transfer function); only the owner can burn
manipulation_risks: REENTRANCY: validatorToken.transferFrom fires BEFORE reserve and totalSupply updates. If validatorToken has callbacks, attacker could reenter mint or other functions with stale pool state to obtain excess LP tokens.
Missing overflow check: pool.reserveValidatorToken + amountValidatorToken could overflow uint128 (only caught by Solidity checked arithmetic, giving confusing error)
Fee-on-transfer tokens: LP receives LP tokens valued at amountValidatorToken but fewer tokens actually deposited

path: 1. Caller invokes burn(userToken, validatorToken, liquidity, to)
5. _calculateBurnAmounts: amountUserToken = liquidity * reserveUserToken / totalSupply; amountValidatorToken = liquidity * reserveValidatorToken / totalSupply
7. IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) — EXTERNAL CALL BEFORE STATE UPDATE
8. IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(to, amountValidatorToken) — EXTERNAL CALL BEFORE STATE UPDATE
9. liquidityBalances[msg.sender] -= liquidity — STATE UPDATE AFTER TRANSFERS
10. totalSupply -= liquidity — STATE UPDATE AFTER TRANSFERS
11. pool.reserveUserToken -= amountUserToken — STATE UPDATE AFTER TRANSFERS
12. pool.reserveValidatorToken -= amountValidatorToken — STATE UPDATE AFTER TRANSFERS
transformations: Proportional share: amount = liquidity * reserve / totalSupply
data_flows.trust_changes: CRITICAL: External calls happen while all state (balances, reserves, totalSupply) still reflects pre-burn values
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL REENTRANCY: Both token transfers occur BEFORE any state updates. If userToken or validatorToken has callback hooks (ERC777, hooks, etc.), the attacker can reenter burn(). On reentry, liquidityBalances[msg.sender] is unchanged, totalSupply is unchanged, and reserves are unchanged. The exact same liquidity amount can be burned again, extracting double (or more) the tokens. This completely drains the pool.
Division by zero if totalSupply somehow reaches 0 (mitigated by MIN_LIQUIDITY lock but worth noting)
Rounding down on both token amounts means dust is left in the pool on each burn

core_rules: Fixed-rate swap: FeeSwap charges 0.3% fee (M=9970), RebalanceSwap charges 0.15% fee (N=9985)
Single-sided LP: only validatorToken is deposited; userToken accumulates from FeeSwaps
MIN_LIQUIDITY (1000 wei) locked permanently on first deposit to prevent total supply reaching zero
LP share calculation accounts for 'effective value' of userToken at N/SCALE discount
Burns proportionally return both tokens based on LP share of totalSupply
economic_incentives: LPs earn spread between FeeSwap fee (0.3%) and RebalanceSwap fee (0.15%) = 0.15% per unit cycle
Rebalancers incentivized: they pay only 0.9985 validatorToken per 1 userToken extracted
No protocol fee extraction — all spread accrues to LPs
Fixed-rate model means no impermanent loss in the traditional sense, but LPs bear inventory risk if rebalancing doesn't occur
governance_mechanisms: No admin controls, no pause functionality, no fee adjustment capability

edge_cases.scenario: CRITICAL: Reentrancy in burn() — transfers before state updates
edge_cases.expected_behavior: LP tokens should be non-reusable; burning X LP tokens should only work once
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Both token transfers (lines: IERC20.transfer) execute BEFORE liquidityBalances, totalSupply, and reserves are decremented. A callback-enabled token (ERC777) allows reentry into burn() where the same LP balance is still available, enabling repeated extraction.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker deploys pool with ERC777-compatible userToken or validatorToken. Provides liquidity. Calls burn() → receives callback → reenters burn() → drains entire pool. Loss = total pool reserves.

edge_cases.scenario: CRITICAL: Reentrancy in executeFeeSwap() — callback before reserve update
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Reserve check should prevent over-extraction of validatorToken
edge_cases.actual_behavior: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom fires BEFORE reserveValidatorToken is decremented. If userToken triggers a callback, attacker reenters executeFeeSwap while reserveValidatorToken still shows the full pre-swap amount, bypassing the sufficiency check repeatedly.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker creates pool with ERC777 userToken and legitimate validatorToken. Calls executeFeeSwap repeatedly via reentrancy callback, draining all validatorToken reserves.

edge_cases.scenario: HIGH: Optimistic state update in rebalanceSwap() enables phantom reserve inflation
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Reserves should reflect actual token balances
edge_cases.actual_behavior: pool.reserveValidatorToken is incremented BEFORE validatorToken.transferFrom actually moves tokens. During the transferFrom callback, the contract state shows inflated validatorToken reserves. Reentering burn() yields proportionally more validatorToken than actually exists.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker with LP tokens calls rebalanceSwap with ERC777 validatorToken. During transferFrom callback, reenters burn(). Burns LP tokens based on inflated reserves. transferFrom then completes but damage is done — pool is insolvent.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Reserves should track actual contract balances
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Reserves are updated by nominal transfer amounts (amountIn/amountOut), not actual received amounts. Fee-on-transfer tokens cause progressive divergence between tracked reserves and actual balances. Eventually, burns and swaps revert when trying to transfer more than the actual balance.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If a pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token, LPs who deposited early can withdraw before the deficit becomes apparent, while later LPs lose funds.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should revert or be a no-op
edge_cases.actual_behavior: amountOut = 0. No explicit check for amountIn == 0. Proceeds to transfer 0 tokens (may revert on some ERC20 implementations). Emits FeeSwap event with 0 amounts.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Can be used for event spam/griefing, wastes gas for callers

edge_cases.scenario: First depositor minting with minimum amount
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Minimum liquidity lock prevents very small first deposits
edge_cases.actual_behavior: MIN_LIQUIDITY = 1000 (in smallest units, ~1e-15 for 18-decimal tokens). First depositor needs only 2002 wei to get 1 LP token. For 18-decimal stablecoins, this is negligible. The protection is extremely weak.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No classic donation-based first depositor attack is possible since reserves use internal accounting rather than balanceOf. However, the locked MIN_LIQUIDITY is economically insignificant.

edge_cases.scenario: Missing overflow check in mint for reserveValidatorToken accumulation
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should explicitly check and revert with descriptive error if reserves overflow uint128
edge_cases.actual_behavior: rebalanceSwap has explicit overflow check for reserveValidatorToken, but mint does not. The uint128 addition will revert due to Solidity 0.8 checked math, but with a generic Panic(0x11) error instead of InsufficientReserves.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No direct exploit; only confusing error message. In practice, uint128.max ≈ 3.4e38 is extremely large for stablecoins.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: Positive rebases increase actual balance without updating reserves — excess tokens stuck forever. Negative rebases decrease actual balance below tracked reserves — burns/swaps revert.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Pools with rebasing tokens become insolvent or trap funds. Users who detect rebase events early can front-run to extract value.

edge_cases.scenario: Burn with pool containing only one token type (reserveUserToken = 0)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: amountUserToken = 0 (liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0). IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) is called, which may revert on some ERC20 implementations that reject zero-value transfers.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: LPs could be unable to exit if userToken reverts on zero transfer. Their funds are locked.

edge_cases.scenario: No slippage protection or deadline on swaps
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Users should be able to specify minimum output and transaction deadline
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Fixed-rate swaps have deterministic output, so slippage from price movement is not possible. However, transactions can be held in mempool indefinitely and executed when it may no longer be desired.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Stale transactions executed at unfavorable times, though fixed rate limits the impact

assumption_violations: CRITICAL ASSUMPTION VIOLATED: No reentrancy guards present. The contract assumes token transfers don't trigger callbacks. Any ERC777, ERC1363, or hook-enabled token breaks this assumption.
Assumes all tokens are standard ERC20 with no fee-on-transfer, rebasing, or non-standard return behavior
Assumes token.transfer and token.transferFrom always return true or revert (no false returns checked — OZ IERC20 interface doesn't use SafeERC20)
Assumes all stablecoins have identical value (1:1 peg); any depeg breaks the fixed-rate swap economics
Assumes tokens do not revert on zero-value transfers
Assumes tokens have the same number of decimals; different decimals break the fixed-rate 1:1 assumption
game_theory_risks: LP value leakage: If rebalancing doesn't occur, userToken accumulates in the pool. LPs are exposed to inventory risk — they deposited validatorToken but may withdraw mostly userToken.
Arbitrage mispricing: The fixed-rate model ignores market prices. If actual market rate diverges from 1:1 minus fees, arbitrageurs will drain the more valuable token.
No incentive for rebalancers when peg holds: rebalanceSwap charges a fee, so rational actors only rebalance when userToken is cheaper than validatorToken elsewhere.
Pool can be permanently drained of validatorToken via executeFeeSwap with no mechanism to replenish other than rebalanceSwap or new LP deposits.
Malicious pool creation: Attacker can create pools with worthless tokens pretending to be stablecoins, tricking users into providing real liquidity.
integration_risks: No SafeERC20 usage — tokens that return false instead of reverting will silently fail, leading to state updates without actual token movement
Non-standard ERC20 tokens (USDT, BNB) that don't return bool will cause the IERC20 interface calls to revert
No token whitelist or validation — pools can be created with any address including contracts that are not ERC20 tokens
No oracle integration to validate token price parity — relies entirely on the assumption that paired tokens are 1:1 stablecoins
Cross-pool interactions: same token can be userToken in one pool and validatorToken in another, creating complex interdependencies
calculation_errors: executeFeeSwap: amountOut = (amountIn * 9970) / 10000 — correctly implements 0.3% fee with truncation (favors pool). No error.

rebalanceSwap: amountIn = (amountOut * 9985) / 10000 + 1 — the +1 ensures minimum payment but creates disproportionate fee for very small swaps (e.g., amountOut=1 → amountIn=1, which is 0% fee instead of 0.15%)
mint first deposit: liquidity = amountValidatorToken / 2 — integer division truncates odd amounts, losing 0.5 wei of 'value'. This is negligible.

mint subsequent: denom uses N*reserveUserToken/SCALE which truncates, slightly undervaluing userToken reserves. This means slightly more LP tokens are minted than strictly correct, marginally diluting existing LPs.
_calculateBurnAmounts: proportional calculation truncates, leaving dust in the pool. Over many burns this dust accumulates. The locked MIN_LIQUIDITY ensures this dust is never orphaned.

token_semantics.notes: User-specified token address with zero validation. Not checked for ERC20 compliance. No SafeERC20 wrapper used. Fee-on-transfer and rebasing tokens break internal reserve accounting. ERC777/ERC1363 tokens enable reentrancy attacks. Non-standard return tokens (e.g. USDT) may cause reverts through IERC20 interface.

User-specified token address with zero validation. Same risks as userToken. Additionally used in optimistic state update pattern in rebalanceSwap (reserves updated before transferFrom), enabling phantom reserve inflation via reentrancy.

Internal LP accounting via liquidityBalances mapping. No transfer function exists — LP positions are non-transferable. MIN_LIQUIDITY (1000) locked on first deposit to prevent total supply reaching zero. Reentrancy in burn allows same LP balance to be burned multiple times.

invariants.name: reserve_balance_consistency
invariants.notes: VIOLATED by: fee-on-transfer tokens (actual < tracked), rebasing tokens (actual != tracked), reentrancy attacks (state update ordering). The contract tracks reserves internally but never reconciles with actual balances.

invariants.name: lp_supply_conservation
invariants.notes: totalSupply = sum(all liquidityBalances) + MIN_LIQUIDITY. MIN_LIQUIDITY is added to totalSupply on first deposit but never assigned to any address, ensuring totalSupply never reaches zero. VIOLATED during reentrancy: burn transfers tokens before decrementing balances, allowing double-burn.

invariants.name: swap_rate_invariant
invariants.notes: Constants: FeeSwap rate = M/SCALE = 0.997, RebalanceSwap rate = SCALE/N ≈ 1.0015 (caller pays). Net LP spread per cycle ≈ 0.15%. Fixed rates regardless of pool state or market conditions.

invariants.name: burn_proportionality
invariants.notes: amountToken = liquidity * reserve / totalSupply. Truncation means slightly less than proportional is returned. Dust accumulates in pool over many burns.
